finding.title: Fix audit result merge/ingest identity, exit-code, and replay defects
finding.summary: Discharge the audit-merge-and-results module contract over dispatch/merge-results.mjs, dispatch/validate.mjs, and src/audit/cli/mergeAndIngestCommand.ts. Key invariants: manifest-reconciled completeness (acceptance judged against runs/<run_id>/pending-audit-tasks.json, never against whichever task-results files happen to exist), result-file selection excludes PACKET_SCHEMA_FILENAME_SET support artifacts, the task-identity gate (a result validates ONLY in the context of its assigned task from the manifest — null/absent task context must hard-fail, never fail-open), identity-is-the-tool's-authority (packet-id-keyed rebinding permitted only for a packet with exactly one still-outstanding task), dedup-by-task_id at ingest (seenTaskIds), the exit-code contract (non-zero ONLY for genuine dispatched failures; budget-capped notDispatched > 0 rounds exit 0), no destructive truncation on re-run (run-results.json written only when passing.length > 0; a blocked no-op throws BEFORE any write), idempotent replay via the merge-complete.json marker with staleness self-heal, ownership-gated terminal handling (peer-reclaimed tasks partitioned to unowned-tasks.json under the AUDIT_TASK_CLAIM_LEASE_MS horizon), and single-payload stdout reporting whose counts partition the pending manifest. Finding cluster: COR-7602834d, COR-6bbc4957, COR-85e4ec9a, COR-9b07685e, COR-5a077210, COR-44bea9c0 (dispatch/merge-results.mjs + dispatch/validate.mjs), and COR-6ba55c63 (mergeAndIngestCommand.ts, shared surface with the planning node). Red-green protocol: write the failing test first per the module's test_validator_plan specs (POSITIVE/NEGATIVE prefixed assertions, e.g. null-task-context rejection, duplicate task_id rejection, exit-status partition), verify red, fix, verify green. Note dispatch/validate.mjs adapts validateAuditResults from built dist/, so npm run build is a runtime precondition for its tests. finding.title: Restrict cycle detection to load-order edges in graph signal extraction
finding.summary: Discharge the graph-and-cycle-extraction module contract over src/audit/extractors/graph.ts, src/audit/extractors/graphSignals.ts, src/audit/extractors/risk.ts — the ARC-c66ed30f fix (theme T-005). Key invariant LOAD-ORDER-EDGES-ONLY: cycle detection and nodesInCycles must consume ONLY structural load-order edge kinds; today allGraphEdges (graphSignals.ts) folds references/heuristics buckets into detectCycles, so a repo-path prose mention or heuristic edge fabricates phantom cycles that corrupt member_of_cycle risk signals. Implement the structuralImportEdges(bundle) selector returning only the imports + calls buckets and route detectCycles / nodesInCycles / hub derivation through it, keeping routes ({path,handler,method}) and GIT_CO_CHANGE_CATEGORY exclusions as a floor, not a ceiling. Preserved invariants: STABLE CONTENT-DERIVED ARRAY ORDER (uniqueSortedEdges from-then-to-then-kind, uniqueSortedRoutes — never readdir/iteration order, so stableStringify content hashes stay churn-free), malformed-input-degrades-to-empty (allGraphEdges drops edges lacking string endpoints; readNodeMetricSignals returns [] on absent/malformed node_metrics), deriveGraphSignals purity/determinism (no IO, no input-bundle mutation; live-bundle and design-assessment executors get byte-identical results), rotation-canonical cycle dedup via canonicalCycleKey (dedupe by rotation, not sort, collapsing DFS-start variants), STRUCTURAL_FAMILY_CAP=2 on member_of_cycle + is_hub + seam_endpoint in buildRiskRegister risk_score, immutable/idempotent merge seams (mergeAnalyzerRiskSignals, deriveRiskConcentration, mergeAnalyzerGraphContribution return NEW values), content-hash-gated edge-cache reuse (path_lookup_hash + per-file content_key in the GraphEdgeCache), and language-neutral construction (edge-kind selection by declared kind/bucket only — python-import, dynamic-import, re-export, route-registration cases stay separately labeled). Red-green protocol: write the failing cycle-detection test first (a references/heuristic edge closing a would-be cycle must NOT produce a cycle after the fix; a genuine imports/calls cycle still must), assert pre-fix red, then fix; add the risk-score assertion that phantom-cycle members lose their member_of_cycle contribution. HEAD-premise check applies (v0.34.7 audited vs v0.34.10 HEAD) though this cluster is not among the shipped v0.34.8-v0.34.10 fixes; verify before implementing.

finding.title: Fix clone-mode finding loss/resurrection in shared cross-lens dedupe
finding.summary: Discharge the shared-findings-dedupe module contract over src/shared/findings/dedupe.ts — the clone-mode dedupe loss/resurrection cluster COR-5c71a9ff and COR-5c71a9ff-2, with drift-guard finding TST-ccf4eccd verified here against the production behavior (its test-side twin TST-286008a5 lands in the tests-and-guards node). Key invariants: Conservation (every input finding appears in the crossLensDedupe output exactly once OR is recorded as absorbed in mergeMap — never both, never neither; no loss, no resurrection), an absorbed finding never acts as a survivor (once removed it is excluded from every subsequent pairwise comparison in BOTH loops), clone-mode canonical accumulation (with survivorMutation:'clone' there is exactly ONE canonical clone per original survivor; every subsequent merge into that survivor mutates the same clone — the repeated-survivor N>=3 case where the current code loses accumulated affected_files/evidence), clone-mode caller isolation (no caller-supplied Finding object is ever mutated), mergeMap references canonical and resolvable (every mergeMap value is the id of a finding present in the returned array; merge chains collapse), idempotence (re-running crossLensDedupe on its own output is a fixpoint: empty mergeMap, findings unchanged), deterministic winner selection (severityRank then confidenceRank, ties keep the earlier i-slot finding), cross-lens vs same-lens vs cross-file separation (cross-file merging ONLY on exact findingReEmissionKey equality), hard categoryGate absolute and layered first, and single normalization/grounding precedence (one trim+lowercase normalization; mergeGrounding precedence lattice). Preserve the exported absorb mechanics (absorbFinding, mergeAffectedFiles, upsertFindingByIdentity, sameLensDedupe) and the onMerge({absorbed, survivor}) callback order, and keep both live policy draws working unchanged: audit's draw in src/audit/reporting/mergeFindings.ts (soft gate, mutate) and remediate's clone-mode draw. finding.title: Circular dependency chain breaks initialization guarantees
finding.summary: The module import graph contains multiple high-order cycles (8+ modules) centered on src/shared/index.ts → src/shared/config/resolveSessionConfig.ts → src/shared/providers/auditorSources.ts → src/shared/providers/providerPathGuard.ts → src/shared/types/sessionConfig.ts → src/remediate/index.ts. The system claims 'language-neutral' and single-sourced architecture, but cycles imply unspecified initialization ordering: which module's exports are available when a dependent module is first imported is undefined. A lazy import or re-export could be valid, but circular dependencies also permit transitive initialization-order bugs where module A.fn() calls B.fn() which calls A.prop that may not yet exist. No import-order contract is documented; no circular-dependency enforcement gate exists at CI time (knip --production does not flag cycles). This leaves the system vulnerable to 'works in CI, fails in production' initialization bugs where order-of-first-import differs from test order.

finding.title: Dispatch result schema contract not enforced across audit/remediate paths
finding.summary: RollingDispatchResult (src/shared/dispatch/rollingDispatch.ts:106-168) is the shared contract for dispatch outcomes across both orchestrators. However, the schema is ONLY type-enforced at compile time; no runtime schema validation is performed on worker results before merging (audit ingest, remediate accept). If a worker (or a middleware) produces a result.outcome value like 'unknown_outcome' (not one of 'success'|'rate_limited'|'timeout'|'error'|'credit_exhausted'|'quota_unclassified'), the calling orchestrator will accept it without error. The 'outcome' field is then used in control-flow branches (handleResult in rolling dispatch, mergeImplementResults in remediate dispatch) that assume outcome is one of the known enum values — an unrecognized outcome would silently fall through to a default case or cause type narrowing to fail. Counterexample: audit-side dispatch-result ingest (ingestResultsCommand.ts) reads worker JSON from disk without deserializing through a Zod schema that validates outcome enum membership. A corrupted result.json with outcome='partial_failure' (a typo) would be accepted and cause undefined behavior downstream. The contract 'outcome must be an enum value' is implicit, never validated at ingestion.

File-lock contract does not guarantee concurrent reader sees written state
entries.rationale: Disapproved by the user at the review gate (review-necessity: strategic).

finding.title: File-lock contract does not guarantee concurrent reader sees written state
finding.summary: The file-lock implementation (src/shared/quota/fileLock.ts) guarantees exclusive write access to a protected path but does NOT contractually guarantee that a reader acquiring the lock after a writer releases it will see the writer's changes. The lock uses token-based validity checks (readFile/check token equality before unlink), but this is a LEASE-model lock, not a synchronization lock. Between a writer releasing the lock (releaseLock calls unlink) and a reader acquiring it, another concurrent process could have acquired-and-released the lock multiple times, or the filesystem could have buffered/delayed a prior write. The caller-supplied fn() in withFileLock (line 214-238) is responsible for the critical I/O (readJsonFile/writeJsonFile), but there is no contract stating 'if fn reads a file inside the lock, it will see writes from prior lock-holders.' Tests pass because single-process runs serialize I/O; multi-process concurrent runners (multiple IDEs, multiple agents) may observe stale reads. This is particularly hazardous for state.json mutation (remediate state store) and quota-state persistence (shared quota ledger) where stale reads cause divergent decisions.

finding.title: File-lock steal protects the lock artifact, not the critical section it guards; late writer silently clobbers
finding.summary: Corroborated across three sources: the adversary perspective (race analysis), the novelty perspective (library adoption), and the prior verdict (lock fragility). `withFileLock` (src/shared/quota/fileLock.ts) treats any lock older than STALE_LOCK_MS (30s) as abandoned and lets a waiter steal it via the `.steal` sidecar — the steal itself is race-free, but nothing checks whether the ORIGINAL holder is still alive: there is no heartbeat/lease renewal on the lock (unlike ReservationLedger/ClaimRegistry, which have `withClaimHeartbeat`). A `mutate()` callback doing arbitrary async I/O can legitimately overrun 30s — `mergeImplementResultsIntoState` (src/remediate/steps/dispatch/marshal.ts) does per-block async contract reads inside `StateStore.mutate()`, realistic to overrun on a large run with Windows fs flakiness — after which a second process steals the lock and starts its own read-modify-write while the first is mid-flight; when the first finishes, `createLockedJsonStore.persist()` calls `writeJsonFile` unconditionally, a blind overwrite with no version/fence check, silently clobbering the second writer. Fix: fence writes with a version/token compare so a stolen-lock holder's late write is rejected, or heartbeat long-held locks / move I/O outside the lock. Additionally (novelty perspective, aligned with the project's own two-tier dependency policy that imported smol-toml/yaml for correctness-sensitive formats): cross-process locking is exactly the correctness-sensitive, not-fully-owned mechanism that policy says to import — evaluate `proper-lockfile` (mkdir-atomic, stale detection, retries) as the substrate rather than continuing to hand-verify the bespoke steal protocol, whose own comments record a prior double-hold bug in this exact mechanism.

finding.title: The charter/systemic/design-review meta-stack layers 5+ LLM-judgment stages before the first real review task
finding.summary: Raised twice independently by the pragmatist perspective (as pipeline ceremony and as a self-referential case study). audit-code's PRIORITY array runs ~24 obligations before a line-level review task executes; the meta-review layers alone stack charter extraction -> charter delta -> design review (contract) -> design review (conceptual, itself a 5-perspective fan-out + judge) -> charter clarification -> systemic-challenge loop (looped until quiescent). Each layer has its own executor, artifact, staleness rule, and drain slot. Individually defensible, cumulatively unweighed: the docs never measure what fraction of a run's tokens/wall-clock goes to pre-review meta-stages versus the per-file review tasks that actually produce findings, and a maintainer debugging 'why didn't my finding show up' must hold 24 sequential concepts. Recommended change (kept as the pragmatist stated it): collapse the meta-review layers into the ordinary review pass as configurable depth rather than separate first-class pipeline stages, roughly halving the obligation count without losing the judgment; instrument the meta-vs-review cost ratio first so the cut is evidence-driven. This finding subsumes the prior verdict's dispatch-opacity concern's root cause at the pipeline level (see DR-010 for the dispatch-local version).
